summary: Armin argues that Firefox removing the global “Disable JavaScript” checkbox is a long-overdue, positive move. He contends that people disable JavaScript mainly for privacy or speed, but blanket disabling doesn’t actually solve either problem. Modern web apps increasingly rely on JavaScript for core functionality, making the old expectation of full non-JS fallbacks unrealistic. He emphasizes that users can’t reliably judge which scripts are necessary and that selective, intelligent blocking is a better approach. The conclusion is that developers and browsers should embrace JavaScript’s central role and focus on smarter tooling rather than global opt-outs.
key_points: Global JavaScript disabling does not meaningfully improve privacy or performance.
Modern applications are often JS-dependent, making no-JS fallbacks impractical.
Users cannot reliably decide which scripts are safe or required for functionality.
Payment flows and third-party integrations frequently require JavaScript to work at all.
The better solution is targeted, intelligent blocking via extensions, not a global switch.
money_quotes: I believe there are two reasons why some people want to disable JavaScript: the feeling of extra privacy and improving page speeds.
None of those things are solved by disabling JavaScript categorically.
A user cannot reliably decide on if the JavaScript is necessary or not.
It’s a great day for a web developer when we can finally assume that a browser will have JavaScript running.
You don’t get extra privacy by disabling JavaScript. I can fully track you even without JavaScript.
Instead of having a global “disable JavaScript and cookies” flag we should instead invest more into things like tightly tuned browser extensions.
JavaScript is quickly becoming a huge part of modern web applications.
key_insight: Blanket disabling of JavaScript is an outdated and ineffective approach; the web now depends on JS, and smarter, selective controls are the real solution.
